texto: Now I missed all this casting, I'm just going to take a look at the lure that we're using, the popper. Now these have been used for a long time to catch all sorts of fish, but there are none better for catching the GT because the Giant Trevelli is such an aggressive predator, and all predators are going to single out any fish that looks weak. So this does two things, it wiggles around like a wounded fish, but almost more importantly, this scoop face here takes a big gulp of air and drags it down and creates a bubble trail. It's visual, it can be seen from a long way away, but it's also audible, it makes a popping, ripping noise, like a fleeing bait fish. There's also another theory, that big gulp that it makes as it sucks down that air also sounds like a Giant Trevelli feeding. Just like any creature, when there's a good feed going on it doesn't want to miss out. Well that's all the theory on how the popper works, now let's see it in action. Rightio, with these it's all about letting the tackle do the work, so that's about the amount of length you want off the rod tip, you don't need too much. It's pretty much like casting anything, except just for safety's sake, don't over rip it, because you can get, as these knots come out, particularly when we start casting, get tangled on here, and then it'll whip around, and there's going to be a guy standing with a camera about there-ish. So just to work your way out, start here and just, you don't need to flick too hard. To work the popper, you wind tight and give it a short jab, see your rod tip stays low, and you can pause in between. So you're basically giving it short jabs down. Don't rip up, otherwise it skips out of the water like that. When you get a bite, the trick is getting that hook in, because sometimes they're quite bony around the mouth, giving it a good pull, making sure there's plenty of drag, because you're going to need it, and keeping a high rod angle when you're hooked up, because they're going to be trying to get down to ground to rub you off, and you're trying to lift his head up, so it's just that jab. And we're going to be doing this lots here. You could go 100 casts between bites, no worries. So don't get too excited at the start. Let the gear do the work. Okay, she's all yours, mate. Go, go, go! Kiwi boy! Back to work! Yee-haw! Follow, follow, follow, follow! Boom! Come on, cast behind, cast behind! Good cast! Yes! Oh, another bite. Oh, there is another, another one, yeah. They smashed that popper. There was two or three GTs there, and I think there was a Barracuda also. I've set the hook! That's a nice fish, eh? He's on fire! My arm's on fire. There's two there. It's right under the water.
